title: Gap Assessment vs Full GMP Audit: Where to Start
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A GMP gap assessment or a full GMP audit? Learn which to choose, how they differ and where to start, with a risk-based decision guide for UK and EU quality teams.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette set against deep navy, with a single subtle green accent. A gloved hand in sterile nitrile holds a transparent clipboard or tablet over a softly blurred GMP cleanroom corridor, polished stainless-steel surfaces and frosted glass catching soft natural daylight from one side. Shallow depth of field draws focus to crisp foreground detail while the background dissolves into elegant bokeh. Calm, precise and premium in mood, evoking inspection readiness and quality assurance. 4:3 aspect ratio, no text, no logos, no readable documents.

faqs.q: Is a GMP gap assessment the same as a self-inspection?
faqs.a: No. A self-inspection under EU GMP Chapter 9 is a periodic, full review of your own quality system to confirm ongoing compliance, and it produces formal findings. A gap assessment is a narrower benchmarking exercise that measures your current state against a defined standard to plan improvement. You can use a gap assessment to prepare for a self-inspection, but it does not replace the documented self-inspection programme regulators expect.

faqs.q: How long does a GMP gap assessment usually take compared with a full audit?
faqs.a: A gap assessment is deliberately focused, so on-site time is often one to two days depending on scope, with a prioritised report following shortly after. A full GMP audit samples records and observes practice across the whole quality system, so it typically requires more preparation, more on-site days and a formal audit plan. The exact duration always depends on site size, complexity and the standards in scope.

faqs.q: Can a gap assessment be used as evidence for supplier qualification?
faqs.a: Generally not on its own. Supplier qualification and quality agreements usually require a documented full audit that provides graded findings and defensible evidence of compliance. A gap assessment can be a useful early screening step, but for formal acceptance of a CMO, API supplier or distributor, a full audit is the expected standard. Always check what your own quality system and customers require.
